content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 323-5662          322-5660             322-5901            322-6441

June 27, 1985


Gordon W. Duffy
Paul Priolo Associates
1228 N Street, Suite 8
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-85-126

Dear Mr. Duffy:

     Thank you for your request for advice concerning your
obligations under the Political Reform Act of 1974.1/  You
served as Chairman of the Air Resources Board and Secretary of
Environmental Affairs from January 1983 to June 1985.  Effective
June 1, 1985, you became a registered lobbyist.  You are married
to Jean M. Duffy, member of the California Assembly.  You asked
generally whether any of the provisions of the Political Reform
Act affected this situation.  In answer, I will make three
general points.  If specific questions arise in the future on a
reporting requirement or on a matter for interpretation, I will
be happy to assist you.

     First, the lobbying provisions of the Act (Sections 86100,
et seq.), apply generally to you in the same manner as they do
to any other lobbyist.  The Act requires that lobbyists file
reports disclosing expenditures, including gifts, that benefit
legislative officials.  Lobbyists are prohibited from making or
arranging for gifts of more than $10 per month to a legislative
official.  However, gifts from an official's spouse are excluded
from the definition of "gift" in the Act.  Section 82028(b)3).
Accordingly, you do not have to disclose, nor are you subject to

_________________________

1/ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government
Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references are to the
Government Code.

Gordon W. Duffy
June 27, 1985
Page 2


the prohibition in Section 86203, with respect to any gifts,
services, etc., related to your marital relationship.  You are
still prohibited from arranging gifts of $10 or more from your
employer to your spouse.

Second, under the financial disclosure provisions of the,
Act (Sections 87200, et seq.), your spouse is required to report
all sources of income of $250 or more on her annual statement of
economic interests.  Income is defined to include the official's
community property interest in the income of her spouse.
Accordingly, your spouse should report as sources of income any
of your clients who pay you $500 or more during the reporting
period.  In addition, since these clients will be considered
sources of income to her, the disqualification provisions of the
Act (Sections 87100, et seq.) may apply in certain
circumstances.

Lastly, you asked about the applicability of the so-called
"revolving door" provisions (Sections 87400, et seq.) to your
situation.  These provisions of the Act apply only to former
state officers who appear for compensation in certain state
quasi-judicial proceedings.  Proceedings before the State
Legislature are specifically exempted.  Section 87400(a).  Thus,
these provisions will not affect your lobbying activities in any
manner.

Please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901 if I can
be of further assistance.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh

State of California
SACRAMENTO

JUL 1 3 1985

GORDON W. DUFFY
Secretary of
Environmental Affairs

May 30, 1985

Barbara Milman, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

I am writing to supplement my letter
of May 20, 1985 to Fair Political Practices
Commission Chairman Dan Stanford. In addition
to the advice requested in that letter, I would
also appreciate your advising me of what
restrictions, if any, the provisions of Government
Code Section 87400 et seq. will impose on me as
a lobbyist, based on my having served as Chairman
of the Air Resources Board and Secretary of
Environmental Affairs from January 1983 to
June 1985.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

Gordon Duffy
Secretary of
Environmental Affairs

1100 Q STREET, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814    (916) 322-5340

State of California
SACRAMENTO

MAY 24 1985

GORDON W. DUFFY
Secretary of
Environmental Affairs

May 20, 1985

Dan Stanford, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
P.O.Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Barbara Milman

Dear Chairman Stanford;

I am currently employed by the State of
California and will be leaving this position to
become a lobbyist effective June 1, 1985. I am
married to Jean M. Duffy, Member of the California
Assembly.

I would appreciate knowing what provisions
there are in code or regulations which affect the
professional relationship between my wife and me.

Thank you for your advice.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Gordon Duffy
Secretary of
Environmental Affairs

1102 Q STREET, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5840

sections.question: You asked generally whether any of the provisions of the Political Reform Act affected this situation.
sections.conclusion: First, the lobbying provisions of the Act apply generally to you in the same manner as they do any other lobbyist. You are still prohibited from arranging gifts of $10 or more from your employer to your spouse. Second, your spouse is required to report all sources of income of $250 or more on her annual statement, including your clients who pay you $500 or more. Third, the 'revolving door' provisions apply only to former state officers who appear for compensation in certain state judicial proceedings, with proceedings before the State Legislature specifically exempted, and thus will not affect your lobbying activities.
sections.question_synthetic: What provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to a former state officer (Chairman of Air Resources Board and Secretary of Environmental Affairs) who is becoming a registered lobbyist and is married to a California Assembly member?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that standard lobbying provisions apply to the requestor; spousal financial disclosure requirements require reporting of clients paying $500+ annually; and revolving door restrictions do not apply to legislative lobbying activities. The requestor is prohibited from arranging gifts to his spouse but is otherwise subject to standard lobbying regulations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes incoming request letters (dated May 20 and May 30, 1985) followed by FPPC response letter dated June 27, 1985. Some text is partially obscured/corrupted in the source document, but key Q/A sections are discernible. Confidence reduced due to text quality issues.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked generally whether any of the provisions of the Political Reform Act affected this situation.

CONCLUSION: First, the lobbying provisions of the Act apply generally to you in the same manner as they do any other lobbyist. You are still prohibited from arranging gifts of $10 or more from your employer to your spouse. Second, your spouse is required to report all sources of income of $250 or more on her annual statement, including your clients who pay you $500 or more. Third, the 'revolving door' provisions apply only to former state officers who appear for compensation in certain state judicial proceedings, with proceedings before the State Legislature specifically exempted, and thus will not affect your lobbying activities.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a former California state executive (married to an Assembly member) regarding his obligations under the Political Reform Act upon becoming a registered lobbyist. The FPPC addresses lobbying provisions, spousal financial disclosure requirements, and confirms that revolving door restrictions do not apply to legislative lobbying.
